title: Fix incorrect multipler for limit field
body:
I want to merge this change because we recently introduced the `values` field with input limit. By accident the query cost was added to incorrect place. This PR fixes this. 

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The changes on `SelectedAttribute` is expected. We don't have `limit` there, and it is not paginated.  (The query cost for this field will be handled separatly)

<!-- Please mention all relevant issue numbers. -->

# Pull Request Checklist

<!-- Please keep this section. It will make the maintainer's life easier. -->

- [ ] Privileged queries and mutations are either absent or guarded by proper permission checks
- [ ] Database queries are optimized and the number of queries is constant
- [ ] Database migrations are either absent or optimized for zero downtime
- [ ] The changes are covered by test cases
- [ ] All new fields/inputs/mutations have proper labels added (`ADDED_IN_X`, `PREVIEW_FEATURE`, etc.)
- [ ] All migrations have proper dependencies
- [ ] All indexes are added concurrently in migrations
- [ ] All RunSql and RunPython migrations have revert option defined

step_plan.summary: Correct the GraphQL query-cost configuration so `Attribute.values(limit)` complexity scales with the `limit` argument, and ensure `SelectedAttribute.values` is not incorrectly multiplied. Add/adjust tests to lock in the expected behavior and prevent regressions.

steps.operation: Fix COST_MAP entries so `Attribute.values` uses the `limit` multiplier and `SelectedAttribute.values` does not
steps.reason: The `Attribute.values` field is a non-connection list that accepts `limit`, so its query-cost should scale with `limit`. The multiplier was mistakenly applied/configured on the wrong field/type (e.g., using `first/last` on `Attribute.values` and/or adding `limit` on `SelectedAttribute.values`, which has no `limit` argument). Updating the map aligns cost calculation with the schema and prevents under/over-counting query complexity.
steps.side_effects: May change computed cost for client queries that use `attribute { values(limit: X) { ... } }`, potentially causing previously-allowed queries to be rejected (or vice versa) based on correct cost accounting. Must ensure the updated cost-map still passes any internal cost-map validation that checks field/arg existence.

steps.operation: Extend/add regression test to assert query-cost scales with `limit` for `Attribute.values(limit: X)` and does not incorrectly multiply for `SelectedAttribute.values`
steps.reason: Provides a failing test for the prior bug (multiplier applied to the wrong place) and prevents regressions. Specifically verifies that the validator reads the correct multiplier argument name (`limit`) from the cost map for `Attribute.values` and that `SelectedAttribute.values` is not treated as limited/paginated.
steps.side_effects: May require adjusting test query strings/fixtures to include an `attribute { values(limit: N) { ... } }` selection and a `selectedAttribute { values { ... } }` selection. Could expose unrelated cost-map inconsistencies if other entries rely on the old behavior.

steps.operation: Verify `validate_query_cost` integration still behaves correctly with the updated cost map and new tests (adjust only if failing)
steps.reason: `validate_query_cost` is the integration entry-point used by API execution/tests. Even if no logic change is intended, confirm that the updated cost-map configuration is correctly wired through validation and that any exceptions raised (e.g., cost-map mismatch, missing args) remain accurate and user-friendly.
steps.side_effects: Unlikely to change runtime behavior beyond ensuring proper propagation of the corrected cost configuration. If modified, could affect how query-cost errors are raised/handled across the API and might require updating additional tests.

steps.operation: Confirm multiplier parsing supports `limit` for `Attribute.values` and adjust parsing only if `limit` is not being applied as expected
steps.reason: This function interprets multiplier argument names from the cost map and extracts numeric values from GraphQL arguments. With the cost map fixed to use `limit`, ensure `limit` is parsed as a numeric multiplier in the same way as `first/last`. Only update if the current implementation mishandles `limit` (e.g., missing name handling, defaulting behavior).
steps.side_effects: Any changes here can affect cost computation for all fields using argument-based multipliers, so it may require running/adjusting broader query-cost validation tests to avoid unintended changes to other fields’ computed costs.
